sources.title: Kebra Nagast
sources.summary: Comprehensive overview of the 14th-century Ethiopian national epic detailing the Solomonic dynasty's origins through Queen Makeda (Sheba) and King Solomon, including the Ark of the Covenant's transfer to Ethiopia by Menelik I.
sources.content: The Kebra Nagast (Ge'ez: ክብረ ነገሥት, kəbrä nägäśt), or The Glory of the Kings, is a 14th-century national epic of Ethiopia, written in Geʽez by Nebure Id Yeshaq of Aksum. In its existing form, the text is at least 700 years old and purports to trace the origins of the Solomonic dynasty, a line of Ethiopian Orthodox Christian monarchs who ruled the country until 1974, to the biblical king, Solomon and the Queen of Sheba. The text contains an account of how the Queen of Sheba (Queen Makeda of Ethiopia) met king Solomon of Jerusalem and about how the Ark of the Covenant came to Ethiopia with their son Menelik I (Menyelek).

evidences.claim: Queen of Sheba visited King Solomon in Jerusalem
evidences.corroboration: The Kebra Nagast expands on the biblical account in 1 Kings 10:1-13, providing detailed narrative of Makeda's visit, her conversion from sun-worship to the God of Israel, and her relationship with Solomon
evidences.notes: The Kebra Nagast provides extensive elaboration on the brief biblical account, adding theological and political dimensions

sources.title: Kebra Nagast: The Queen of Sheba and Her Only Son Menyelek
sources.author: E. A. Wallis Budge
sources.summary: Complete English translation of the Ethiopian epic by Sir E.A. Wallis Budge, documenting the transfer of the Ark of the Covenant from Jerusalem to Ethiopia and establishing the Solomonic line of Ethiopian kings.
sources.content: This volume contains a complete English translation of the famous Ethiopian work, The "Kebra Nagast," i.e. the "Glory of the Kings [of Ethiopia]." This work has been held in peculiar honour in Abyssinia for several centuries, and throughout that country it has been, and still is, venerated by the people as containing the final proof of their descent from the Hebrew Patriarchs, and of the kinship of their kings of the Solomonic line with Christ, the Son of God. The importance of the book, both for the kings and the people of Abyssinia, is clearly shown by the letter that King John of Ethiopia wrote to the late Lord Granville in August, 1872.

evidences.claim: The Ark of the Covenant was transferred from Jerusalem to Ethiopia
evidences.corroboration: Budge's translation provides the full narrative of how Menelik I (son of Solomon and Sheba) brought the Ark to Ethiopia, establishing it as the new center of God's presence
evidences.notes: This translation represents the foundational English version that shaped Western understanding of the Ethiopian tradition

sources.title: The Kebra Nagast (Ethiopia, c. 1300s)
sources.author: John Terry
sources.summary: Academic analysis of the Kebra Nagast as political ideology supporting the concept of a sacred Solomonic dynasty in Ethiopia, with excerpts from Budge's translation.
sources.content: The Kebra Nagast, often known as the "national epic of Ethiopia," is a long text written in Ge'ez in the 1300s which details the history of Ethiopian monarchs who descend from King Solomon. This narrative is an invention of the fourteenth century meant to support the concept of a "sacred dynasty" anointed by God. The excerpts we'll focus on most closely involve the relationship between Solomon and Makeda (the Queen of Sheba in the southern Arabian Peninsula), the rise of their son Menelik, the transporting of the Ark of the Covenant to his kingdom in Ethiopia, and the eventual conversion of Ethiopia (the kingdom of Axum) to Christianity in the 300s.

evidences.claim: The Kebra Nagast functions as political ideology legitimizing Ethiopian monarchy
evidences.corroboration: This academic analysis frames the text as a 14th-century invention to support the concept of a sacred Solomonic dynasty, showing how biblical narrative was adapted for political purposes
evidences.notes: Provides critical academic perspective on the text's function as political theology rather than mere history

sources.title: Orthodox Tewahedo biblical canon
sources.summary: Overview of the 81-book biblical canon used by Ethiopian and Eritrean Orthodox churches, the largest in Christendom, including unique books like Enoch, Jubilees, and the three Meqabyan.
sources.content: The Orthodox Tewahedo biblical canon is a version of the Christian Bible used in the two Oriental Orthodox Churches of the Ethiopian and Eritrean traditions: the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church and the Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church. At 81 books, it is the largest and most varied biblical canon in traditional Christendom. The Orthodox Tewahedo narrower Old Testament canon contains the entire established Hebrew protocanon. Moreover, with the exception of the first two books of Maccabees, the Orthodox Tewahedo canon also contains the entire Catholic deuterocanon. Unique to the Orthodox Tewahedo canon are the Paralipomena of Jeremiah (4 Baruch), Jubilees, Enoch, and the three books of Meqabyan.

evidences.claim: Ethiopian canon includes three books of Meqabyan distinct from Greek Maccabees
evidences.corroboration: Article clarifies that 1, 2, and 3 Meqabyan (sometimes called Ethiopian Maccabees) are entirely different from the four Greek Books of Maccabees found in Catholic and Eastern Orthodox canons
evidences.notes: Important distinction showing Ethiopian canon developed independently with unique compositions

sources.title: Meqabyan
sources.summary: Detailed description of the three Ethiopian Maccabees books found only in the Ethiopian Orthodox canon, composed in Ge'ez and entirely different from the Greek Maccabees.
sources.content: Meqabyan (Amharic: መቃብያን, romanized: Mek'abiyan, also transliterated as Makabian or Mäqabeyan), also referred to as Ethiopian Maccabees and Ethiopic Maccabees, are three books found only in the Ethiopian Orthodox Old Testament Biblical canon. The language of composition of these books is Geʽez, also called Classical Ethiopic, although they are more commonly found in Amharic today. These books are entirely different in their scope, content and subject from the more well-known books of Maccabees found in Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Bibles.

evidences.claim: The three books of Meqabyan are unique Ethiopian compositions
evidences.corroboration: Article emphasizes that these books are entirely different in scope, content, and subject from Greek Maccabees, representing uniquely Ethiopian religious literature
evidences.notes: Establishes the independent development of Ethiopian canon with indigenous compositions

sources.title: The Bible and its Canon in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church
sources.author: Mikre Sellassie
sources.summary: Academic paper examining the fluid concept of canonicity in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, which traditionally recognizes 81 books without the rigid protocanonical/deuterocanonical distinction.
sources.content: The number of books of the holy Scriptures generally accepted in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church is eighty-one. Thus, all the clergy and laity commonly use the term "eighty and one" whenever they speak of the Bible in their religious encounter. It is not, therefore, surprising to note that the Bible with 66 books is considered incomplete by the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, who widely recognises all the "eighty and one" books on equal basis as divinely inspired without any distinction. The earliest sources for the Canon of the Scriptures of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church are the Fetha Negest and the Sinodos.

evidences.claim: Ethiopian Orthodox Church views all 81 books as equally divinely inspired
evidences.corroboration: Paper explains that the Ethiopian Church does not distinguish between protocanonical and deuterocanonical books, viewing all 81 books as equally inspired without hierarchical categorization
evidences.notes: Shows fundamentally different approach to canonicity compared to Western Christian traditions

sources.title: How Many Books are in the Bible? Qualitative Numbers, or Math for Biblical Scholars
sources.summary: Analysis of the Ethiopian Orthodox canon's complexity, noting that while 81 books is the consensus, the actual composition varies between narrower and broader canons with different counting methods.
sources.content: The Ethiopian Orthodox Church is known for having a larger collection of scriptural books than other Jewish and Christian communities—including, for instance, Enoch and Jubilees—but in the long history of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, Selassie was the first ever to pronounce any biblical collection "complete." A key official statement is a 13th-c. canon law code, The Law of the Kings, which claims to trace its authority to the apostles. This code is clear: the "Divine Books which must be accepted by the Holy Church are Eighty-One in Number." A catalogue of these books follows. It lists only seventy-three. Later Ethiopic commentaries solve this problem by listing additional texts and dividing others into separate books.

evidences.claim: The Ethiopian canon has narrower and broader versions with different compositions
evidences.corroboration: Article explains that the 81-book total is achieved through different methods: some lists divide books (like Proverbs into two) while others add ecclesiastical rulebooks to reach the number
evidences.notes: Demonstrates the fluidity and complexity of Ethiopian canon formation

sources.title: The Qumran Jubilees Manuscripts as Evidence for the Literary Growth of the Book
sources.author: Eibert Tigchelaar
sources.summary: Scholarly analysis of Qumran Jubilees manuscripts (4Q216, 4Q217, 4Q221) as evidence for textual development and revision of Jubilees during the first century BCE, challenging assumptions of a fixed text.
sources.content: Whereas scholarship generally assumes a fixed text of Jubilees by the second century B.C.E., this article explores the evidence for first-century B.C.E. revisions and Fortschreibungen. The combined evidence of 4Q216 and 4Q217 hints at a first-century B.C.E. addition to Jub. 1, and the absence of Jub. 23:32 from 4Q176 frag. 21 may indicate subsequent Fortschreibung. The article argues, following Milik, for a manuscript bisection of Jubilees, and for a reassignment of 4Q176 frag. 21 to 4Q221. The manuscript remains of 4Q216 consists of fragments from two consecutive sheets, sewn together, but written in two different scribal hands.

evidences.claim: Jubilees underwent textual revision during the Qumran period
evidences.corroboration: Analysis of 4Q216 and related manuscripts shows evidence of first-century BCE revisions and additions to the text, indicating literary development rather than a fixed text
evidences.notes: Important paleographic evidence for the dynamic textual history of Jubilees

sources.title: 4Q216 Rethinking Jubilees in the First Century BCE
sources.author: Matthew P. Monger
sources.summary: Material philological analysis of 4Q216, the oldest extant Jubilees manuscript, challenging assumptions about textual stability and complete copies at Qumran.
sources.content: 4Q216, also known as 4QJubileesa, is the oldest extant manuscript containing text from what is today known as the Book of Jubilees. As the signum implies, this manuscript is among the manuscripts found in what has been labelled Qumran Cave 4, close to the ruins of Khirbet Qumran, on the Western shore of the Dead Sea. The importance of this manuscript has been evident since its preliminary and official publication, where the antiquity of Jubilees as a work and its textual stability have been highlighted. These initial publications relied heavily on classical textual criticism and presented the text of the fragments of 4Q216 within the framework of a reconstructed and retroverted text, giving the impression of a stable text with few textual or literary differences from other extant manuscripts of Jubilees.

evidences.claim: 4Q216 is the oldest known manuscript of the Book of Jubilees
evidences.corroboration: Establishes 4Q216 as the earliest textual witness to Jubilees, dated to the last quarter of the second century BCE or first half of the first century BCE, providing crucial evidence for the text's early transmission
evidences.notes: Fundamental primary source for understanding Jubilees' textual history

sources.title: Sacred Times: The Book of Jubilees at Qumran
sources.summary: Examination of Jubilees' 364-day solar calendar and its significance for understanding Qumran sectarian practices and the broader Second Temple Jewish calendrical debates.
sources.content: Jubilees has a very specific calendar. According to Jub. 6:32, the Israelites are only to recognize a 364-day (solar) year. Other texts throughout the book reflect this view, especially in the creation account. The author says, "And God appointed the sun to be a great sign on the earth for days and for sabbaths and for months and for feasts and for years and for sabbaths of years and for jubilees and for all seasons of the years." The origins of the 364-day calendar have been much debated in the scholarly literature. The most important contributor is Annie Jaubert. Her work with the time scheme in Jubilees led her to apply its calendrical system to the entire Hebrew Bible, and even to the New Testament.

evidences.claim: Jubilees advocates a 364-day solar calendar that structures all sacred time
evidences.corroboration: Paper explains how Jubilees' calendar system organizes festivals, Sabbaths, and jubilees in a fixed solar pattern that differs from lunar calendars, reflecting Qumran sectarian practices
evidences.notes: Crucial for understanding the calendrical distinctiveness of Qumran and Jubilees

sources.title: 4Q216 (transcription)
sources.summary: Digital transcription of the 4Q216 Jubilees manuscript fragments, providing direct access to the Hebrew text of the oldest known Jubilees witness from Qumran Cave 4.
sources.content: 4Q216 is the oldest known manuscript that contains text from what is otherwise only fully known from the Ethiopic maṣḥafa kufāle (Ethiopic Book of Jubilees = EJ). Dated to the last quarter of the second century BCE or first half of the first century BCE, 4Q216 has served as an important witness to the early Hebrew text of Jubilees. As there is a gap of at least 1,500 years between the assumed date of the composition of Jubilees and the earliest EJ manuscripts, the Qumran manuscripts provided important information for understanding the transmission of the text.

evidences.claim: The Hebrew text of 4Q216 provides direct evidence for the early form of Jubilees
evidences.corroboration: Digital transcription allows direct examination of the paleo-Hebrew text fragments, showing the actual wording and structure of Jubilees as preserved at Qumran
evidences.notes: Primary textual evidence for scholarly reconstruction of Jubilees' original Hebrew form

sources.title: The Last of the Rephaim: Conquest and Cataclysm in the Heroic Ages of Ancient Israel
sources.summary: Dissertation exploring giants in biblical narrative, focusing on Nephilim, Anaqim, Rephaim, and their role in Israelite symbolic geography and theology of chaos maintenance.
sources.content: This dissertation explores the role of giants in the narrative and historiographic worlds of symbol, geography, and religion in ancient Israel. The Nephilim, Anaqim, Rephaim, Emim, Zamzumim/Zuzim, some Gibborim, and other individuals (e.g., Goliath) can all be classified as "giants"—not only with respect to their height and other physical properties, but also with respect to the negative moral qualities assigned to giants in antiquity. All that is overgrown or physically monstrous represents a connection to the primeval chaos that stands as a barrier to creation and right rule. In this sense, giants represent chaos-fear, and their eradication is a form of chaos maintenance by both human and divine forces.

evidences.claim: Biblical giants represent chaos that must be eradicated for divine order
evidences.corroboration: Dissertation argues that giants symbolize primeval chaos and their removal represents chaos maintenance, explaining why their extermination is central to conquest narratives
evidences.notes: Provides theological framework for understanding giant traditions in biblical literature

sources.title: THE NEPHILIM: A TALL STORY?
sources.summary: Academic article examining Nephilim in Old Testament and Second Temple literature, arguing they are primarily associated with the antediluvian era and linked to postdiluvian heroes to highlight pre-flood perversity.
sources.content: The Nephilim figure prominently in some popular literature. Their portrayal is speculative, but also based on Second Temple texts, which portray the Nephilim as the giant offspring of angels and human women who were responsible for the corruption that resulted in the flood. The OT includes few direct references to the Nephilim (Gen. 6:4; Num. 13:33; possibly Ezek. 32:27), though they have been generally linked with giant pre-conquest inhabitants of Canaan, particularly Anakites and Rephaim. The lack of detail in the OT suggests the existence of underlying extra-biblical traditions, though substantial differences appear to rule out Second Temple texts as a source for OT writers.

evidences.claim: Nephilim in Numbers 13:33 are linked to antediluvian giants but not necessarily endorsed by the narrator
evidences.corroboration: Article argues that the reference to Nephilim in Numbers 13:33 reflects Israelite tradition but does not mean the biblical narrator endorsed the validity of identifying Canaanite giants with antediluvian Nephilim
evidences.notes: Careful distinction between biblical text and later interpretive traditions

sources.title: The Riddle of the Rephaim
sources.summary: Examination of Rephaim in biblical and Ugaritic sources, showing their multiple identities as shades of the dead, giants, demigods, and their transformation in monotheistic context.
sources.content: The identification of the beings known as "Rephaim" in biblical and ancient Near Eastern sources has caused much bewilderment throughout the years. Biblical dictionaries and encyclopedias usually provide two main meanings for the word: (1) ghosts or shades of the dead, and (2) a mythical and ancient race of giants. These meanings are mostly derived from the mentions of the Rephaim in the Bible. If we go over the dozens of references to the Rephaim in the Bible, we see that it is very difficult to reach a single clear conclusion about their identity. Luckily, we have other sources from the ancient Near East that mention them. The first source is the Ugaritic texts, written in alphabetic cuneiform.

evidences.claim: Rephaim have multiple identities in ancient Near Eastern tradition
evidences.corroboration: Article shows Rephaim appear as shades of the dead, giants, demigods, healers, ancestors, kings, and household gods in various sources, reflecting complex ancient Near Eastern conceptions of heroic dead
evidences.notes: Demonstrates the complexity of giant traditions in ancient Near Eastern religion

sources.title: The Problems of Sons of Gods, Daughters of Humans, and the Nephilim in Genesis 6:1–4: A Reassessment
sources.summary: Reassessment of Genesis 6:1-4 arguing the Nephilim are primarily linked to conquest narratives and giant traditions rather than presupposing flood narrative knowledge.
sources.content: This article engages with earlier scholarly discussions on Genesis 6:1–4 and proposes that, contrary to the majority view, Genesis 6:1–4 does not presuppose knowledge of the flood narrative. Instead, its primary literary role is to introduce the Nephilim in anticipation of the forthcoming land promise and conquest narrative. I argue that the Nephilim are not necessarily read as divinely inspired beings but are instead associated with other pre-Israelite settlers characterized by their immense size. The inconsistent biblical depiction of the promised land—including the Transjordanian territory—and the conflation of various giant terms further suggest that the Nephilim in Genesis 6:4 are linked to the broader conquest narrative and associated traditions, particularly the eradication of giant groups such as the Rephaim.

evidences.claim: Genesis 6:1-4 primarily introduces Nephilim for conquest narrative rather than flood context
evidences.corroboration: Article reinterprets Genesis 6:1-4 as preparation for conquest narratives about giant eradication, showing how Nephilim, Anakim, Emim, Zamzumim, and Rephaim are conflated in Deuteronomic tradition
evidences.notes: Challenges traditional reading that prioritizes flood narrative context
